title: Check if array is palindrome
description: Given an array check if it reads the same forward and backward

examples.explanation: Array length n=5. i=0: j from 4 to 0, find j=4 (since 4=5-1-0), compare arr[0]=1 with arr[4]=1 → match. i=1: j from 4 to 0, find j=3 (since 3=5-1-1), compare arr[1]=2 with arr[3]=2 → match. i=2: j from 4 to 0, find j=2 (since 2=5-1-2), compare arr[2]=3 with arr[2]=3 → match. i=3: j from 4 to 0, find j=1 (since 1=5-1-3), compare arr[3]=2 with arr[1]=2 → match. i=4: j from 4 to 0, find j=0 (since 0=5-1-4), compare arr[4]=1 with arr[0]=1 → match. All comparisons match → return True.
Array length n=5. i=0: j from 4 to 0, find j=4, compare arr[0]=1 with arr[4]=5 → mismatch → return False immediately.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Compare each element with its corresponding element from the end by iterating through the array and checking all pairs. This method is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Palindrome Check
ladders.desc: Complete O(n²) solution to check if an array reads the same forward and backward
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the palindrome array problem. The intuition is straightforward: we need to verify that every element matches its corresponding element from the opposite end. The brute force approach does this by explicitly comparing each element with every possible corresponding element from the end, resulting in O(n²) time complexity. The algorithm works by using nested loops: the outer loop iterates through each element from the start, and the inner loop finds the corresponding element from the end. For each position i, we need to find the element at position n-1-i (where n is array length). The brute force method doesn't directly calculate this but instead searches for it, making it inefficient. Step-by-step: 1) Get array length n. 2) For i from 0 to n-1: a) For j from n-1 down to 0: b) If j equals n-1-i, compare arr[i] with arr[j]. c) If they don't match, return False. 3) If all comparisons pass, return True. Time complexity is O(n²) because for each of n elements, we potentially search through n positions. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to L1 by being the complete implementation that uses the key comparison subroutine. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, not handling empty arrays correctly, and confusing when to return True/False. The real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def is_palindrome_brute(arr): n = len(arr); for i in range(n): for j in range(n-1, -1, -1): if j == n-1-i: if arr[i] != arr[j]: return False; break; return True

examples.trace: Array length n=5. i=0: j from 4 to 0, find j=4 (since 4=5-1-0), compare arr[0]=1 with arr[4]=1 → match. i=1: j from 4 to 0, find j=3 (since 3=5-1-1), compare arr[1]=2 with arr[3]=2 → match. i=2: j from 4 to 0, find j=2 (since 2=5-1-2), compare arr[2]=3 with arr[2]=3 → match. i=3: j from 4 to 0, find j=1 (since 1=5-1-3), compare arr[3]=2 with arr[1]=2 → match. i=4: j from 4 to 0, find j=0 (since 0=5-1-4), compare arr[4]=1 with arr[0]=1 → match. All comparisons match → return True.
Array length n=5. i=0: j from 4 to 0, find j=4, compare arr[0]=1 with arr[4]=5 → mismatch → return False immediately.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find and Compare Corresponding Elements
ladders.desc: For a given index i, find the element at position n-1-i and compare it with arr[i]
ladders.explanation: This is the key subroutine in the brute force approach. The intuition is that to check if an array is a palindrome, for each element at position i, we need to compare it with the element at the mirror position from the end, which is n-1-i. However, in the brute force method, instead of directly accessing arr[n-1-i], we search for this position using an inner loop to emphasize the inefficiency. The algorithm: Given array arr and index i, 1) Calculate n = len(arr). 2) For j from n-1 down to 0: a) If j equals n-1-i, compare arr[i] with arr[j]. b) If they don't match, return False; otherwise, break. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(n) in the worst case as we might search through all positions. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 by being the core operation repeated for each i in the full solution. Common mistakes include: not breaking after finding the correct j, incorrect calculation of mirror index, or not handling the case when i is out of bounds. The real code pattern: def compare_corresponding(arr, i): n = len(arr); for j in range(n-1, -1, -1): if j == n-1-i: if arr[i] != arr[j]: return False; break; return True

examples.trace: n=5, target j = n-1-i = 5-1-1 = 3. Loop j from 4 to 0: j=4 (not 3), j=3 (equals 3) → compare arr[1]=2 with arr[3]=2 → match → break and return True.
n=5, target j = n-1-0 = 4. Loop j from 4 to 0: j=4 (equals 4) → compare arr[0]=1 with arr[4]=5 → mismatch → return False immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Mirror Index Calculation and Comparison
ladders.desc: Understand that element at i must equal element at n-1-i for palindrome property
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core logical insight behind palindrome checking. The intuition is that a palindrome reads the same forward and backward, which mathematically means that for any position i, the element must equal the element at the mirror position from the end. The mirror position is calculated as n-1-i, where n is the array length. This works because array indices start at 0, so the first element (index 0) should match the last (index n-1), the second (index 1) should match the second-to-last (index n-2), and so on. The algorithm to verify this for a single pair: 1) Given array arr and index i, 2) Calculate mirror_index = len(arr) - 1 - i, 3) Compare arr[i] with arr[mirror_index], return True if equal, False otherwise. Time complexity is O(1) for direct access, but in the brute force context, we simulate searching for it. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 as the conceptual basis for finding the corresponding element. Common mistakes include: incorrect mirror index formula (e.g., using n-i instead of n-1-i), not checking if mirror_index is valid, or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. The real code pattern: def check_mirror(arr, i): n = len(arr); mirror = n - 1 - i; return arr[i] == arr[mirror]

examples.trace: n=5, mirror_index = 5-1-1 = 3. arr[1]=2, arr[3]=2 → equal → return True.
n=5, mirror_index = 5-1-2 = 2. arr[2]=3, arr[2]=3 → equal → return True. Note: for odd-length arrays, middle element compares with itself.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Through Array Indices
ladders.desc: Loop through all indices of an array from start to end
ladders.explanation: This is an intermediate concept needed for the brute force solution: iterating through all positions in an array. The intuition is that to check each element in the array, we need to visit every index from the first to the last. In programming, this is typically done with a loop that goes from 0 to n-1, where n is the array length. The algorithm: 1) Get the length n of the array. 2) Use a loop with index i ranging from 0 to n-1 inclusive. 3) For each i, perform some operation (in our case, compare with mirror element). Time complexity is O(n) for the iteration itself. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 because we need to apply the mirror comparison for each index i. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors (e.g., going to n instead of n-1), modifying the array while iterating, or using the wrong loop type. The real code pattern: def iterate_array(arr): n = len(arr); for i in range(n): # do something with arr[i]

examples.trace: n=3. Loop i from 0 to 2: i=0 → access arr[0]=10, i=1 → access arr[1]=20, i=2 → access arr[2]=30.
n=1. Loop i from 0 to 0: i=0 → access arr[0]=5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access Array Element by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve the value stored at a specific position in an array
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation used throughout the solution: accessing an element in an array using its index. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and each position has a numerical index starting from 0. To get an element, you specify the index, and the system returns the value at that position. The algorithm: Given an array arr and an index i, 1) Check if i is within valid bounds (0 to len(arr)-1). 2) If valid, return arr[i]; otherwise, handle error (e.g., index out of bounds). Time complexity is O(1) for direct access. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 because when iterating through indices, we need to access each element. Common mistakes include: using 1-based indexing (assuming first element is at index 1), accessing beyond array bounds, or confusing index with value. The real code pattern: element = arr[index]

examples.trace: Array has indices 0,1,2. Index 1 is valid → access arr[1] which is 10.
Array has index 0 only. Index 0 is valid → access arr[0] which is 100.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is an Array?
ladders.desc: Understand that an array is an ordered collection of elements accessible by position
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding what an array is. An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a collection of elements in a specific order. The intuition is that it's like a row of boxes, each holding a value, and each box has a number (index) starting from 0. Key properties: 1) Ordered: Elements have a fixed sequence. 2) Indexed: Each element can be accessed using its numerical position. 3) Homogeneous: Typically all elements are of the same type (in statically-typed languages). 4) Fixed size: The length is determined at creation (in many languages). This concept works because it provides a way to organize data so we can refer to individual items by position. There's no algorithm here per se, but understanding how to declare, initialize, and think about arrays. Time/space analysis isn't applicable at this basic level. This connects to L4 as the foundation for accessing elements. Common mistakes include: thinking indices start at 1, confusing arrays with other collections like lists, or not understanding that arrays have a fixed order. The real code pattern: In Python-style, arr = [1, 2, 3] creates an array with three elements.

examples.trace: The array has 3 elements in order: first is 'apple' at index 0, second is 'banana' at index 1, third is 'cherry' at index 2.
The array has 1 element: '10' at index 0.

testCases.input: What is at index 1 in [a, b, c]?
What is first element of [x, y, z]?
What is index of 'dog' in [cat, dog, fish]?

approaches.name: Two Pointer O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers starting at the beginning and end of the array, moving inward while comparing elements. This approach is efficient and commonly used for palindrome checks in arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Array is Palindrome Using Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine if an array reads the same forward and backward using two pointers.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that a palindrome array must have symmetric elements: the first element equals the last, the second equals the second-last, and so on. By using two pointers—one starting at the beginning (left) and one at the end (right)—we can compare pairs of elements while moving inward. This avoids unnecessary comparisons and works in linear time with constant space. The algorithm initializes left to 0 and right to n-1, where n is the array length. It then enters a loop while left < right, comparing arr[left] and arr[right]. If they differ, return false immediately. Otherwise, increment left and decrement right. If the loop completes without mismatches, return true. This is efficient because it only requires O(n) time (each element is checked at most once) and O(1) space (only two pointers are used). Connection to L1: This builds on the key subroutine of comparing elements with pointers. Common mistakes include not handling empty or single-element arrays (which are trivially palindromes), off-by-one errors in pointer updates, or incorrect loop conditions. Real code pattern: def is_palindrome(arr): left, right = 0, len(arr)-1; while left < right: if arr[left] != arr[right]: return False; left += 1; right -= 1; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize left=0, right=4. Compare arr[0]=1 and arr[4]=1 → equal. Step 2: Update left=1, right=3. Compare arr[1]=2 and arr[3]=2 → equal. Step 3: Update left=2, right=2. Since left=right, loop condition left<right is false, exit loop. Step 4: Return true.
Step 1: Initialize left=0, right=4. Compare arr[0]=1 and arr[4]=5 → not equal. Step 2: Return false immediately.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compare Array Elements with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Implement the core comparison logic using two pointers to check symmetric positions.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that for palindrome checking, we need to verify symmetry by comparing elements from opposite ends. This subroutine focuses on the iterative comparison process without the full problem wrapper. It takes an array and two indices (left and right) as input, compares the elements at these positions, and updates the pointers. The algorithm: while left < right, if arr[left] != arr[right], return false; else, left += 1 and right -= 1. If the loop finishes, return true. This subroutine is the heart of the full solution, handling the actual element checks. Time complexity is O(n) in the worst case (if all comparisons are made), but it can exit early on mismatch. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called within the full problem's loop. Connection to L2: It relies on the core logic of pointer movement. Common mistakes include not validating input indices or forgetting to update pointers. Real code pattern: def compare_with_pointers(arr, left, right): while left < right: if arr[left] != arr[right]: return False; left += 1; right -= 1; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=4, arr[0]=1, arr[4]=1 → equal. Update left=1, right=3. Step 2: left=1, right=3, arr[1]=2, arr[3]=2 → equal. Update left=2, right=2. Step 3: left=2, right=2, condition left<right is false, exit loop. Return true.
Step 1: left=0, right=4, arr[0]=1, arr[4]=5 → not equal. Return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Move Two Pointers Inward While Comparing
ladders.desc: Understand the technique of incrementing and decrementing pointers to traverse an array from both ends.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that by moving two pointers symmetrically inward, we can efficiently access and compare pairs of elements without extra data structures. This core logic involves initializing two pointers, then in a loop, performing an operation (e.g., comparison) and updating the pointers: left increases, right decreases. The key insight is that this reduces the problem size by two elements per iteration, making it O(n/2) in best case. The algorithm: left = start, right = end; while left < right: # do something with arr[left] and arr[right]; left += 1; right -= 1. This is fundamental for palindrome checks and other symmetric problems. Time complexity depends on the operation inside the loop, but pointer updates are O(1). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is used in the comparison subroutine. Connection to L3: It builds on array indexing. Common mistakes include infinite loops if pointers are not updated or incorrect loop conditions. Real code pattern: left, right = 0, len(arr)-1; while left < right: # access arr[left] and arr[right]; left += 1; right -= 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize left=0, right=4. Step 2: In loop, since left<right (0<4), update left=1, right=3. Step 3: In next iteration, left<right (1<3), update left=2, right=2. Step 4: Now left=2, right=2, condition left<right is false, exit loop.
Step 1: Initialize left=0, right=2. Step 2: In loop, left<right (0<2), update left=1, right=1. Step 3: Now left=1, right=1, condition left<right is false, exit loop.

ladders.title: Building Block — Access Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Learn how to retrieve elements from an array using indices, including from the start and end.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that arrays are indexed collections, and accessing elements by position is fundamental for algorithms. This building block involves understanding zero-based indexing: the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. To access from the end, use index n-1 for the last element, n-2 for the second-last, etc. The algorithm: given an array arr and an index i, return arr[i]. For negative indices or out-of-bounds access, handle errors. This is crucial for the two-pointer approach because we need to compare arr[left] and arr[right]. Time complexity is O(1) for access, space is O(1). Connection to L2: This enables pointer-based element retrieval. Connection to L4: It relies on basic array operations. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using index n instead of n-1 for the last element) or not checking bounds. Real code pattern: def get_element(arr, index): if 0 <= index < len(arr): return arr[index]; else: raise IndexError.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check index 2 is within bounds (0 to 3). Step 2: Access arr[2], which is 30. Return 30.
Step 1: Check index 0 is within bounds (0 to 2). Step 2: Access arr[0], which is 5. Return 5.

testCases.expected: Error: index out of bounds (assuming no negative indexing)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Over an Array with a Loop
ladders.desc: Use a loop to traverse each element in an array sequentially.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that arrays are linear data structures, and processing them often requires visiting each element one by one. This basic operation involves using a loop (e.g., for or while) to access elements from start to end. The algorithm: initialize an index i = 0; while i < len(arr): # do something with arr[i]; i += 1. This is foundational for many algorithms, including the two-pointer approach where we might iterate partially. Time complexity is O(n) for full traversal, space is O(1). Connection to L3: Iteration is used to access elements by index. Connection to L5: It builds on the concept of arrays. Common mistakes include infinite loops if the index is not incremented or off-by-one errors in the loop condition. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): # process arr[i].

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1, process 1. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=2, process 2. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=3, process 3. Step 4: i=3, loop condition i<3 is false, exit.
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=10, process 10. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=20, process 20. Step 3: i=2, loop condition i<2 is false, exit.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is an Array?
ladders.desc: Understand the basic definition and properties of an array as a collection of elements.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that an array is a fundamental data structure that stores multiple items in a contiguous block of memory, allowing efficient access by position. This foundation covers what an array is: an ordered collection of elements, each identified by an index starting from 0. Key properties include fixed size (in some languages) or dynamic resizing, homogeneous or heterogeneous data types, and constant-time access to elements by index. The algorithm: conceptually, an array can be represented as a list like [1, 2, 3], where 1 is at index 0, 2 at index 1, etc. This is prerequisite for all higher levels because the two-pointer approach operates on arrays. Time and space analysis is not applicable here as it's a conceptual understanding. Connection to L4: Arrays are the structure we iterate over. Common mistakes include confusion with other data structures like lists or misunderstanding indexing. Real code pattern: In Python, an array can be a list: arr = [1, 2, 3].

examples.trace: Step 1: The array has 3 elements. Step 2: Index 0 corresponds to the first element, 10. Step 3: Index 1 corresponds to the second element, 20. Step 4: Index 2 corresponds to the third element, 30.
Step 1: The array has 1 element. Step 2: Index 0 corresponds to the only element, 5.

approaches.name: Reverse and Compare O(n)
approaches.summary: Create a reversed copy of the array and compare it element-by-element with the original. This method is intuitive but uses additional space for the reversed array.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Array is Palindrome Using Reverse and Compare
ladders.desc: Complete solution that creates a reversed copy of the array and compares it element-by-element with the original.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the palindrome checking problem by leveraging a simple but effective technique: create a reversed version of the array and compare it with the original. The intuition is that if an array reads the same forward and backward, then the reversed array should be identical to the original. This method is straightforward and mirrors how one might check a palindrome manually—by writing it backwards and seeing if it matches. The algorithm proceeds in two main steps: first, create a reversed copy of the input array by iterating from the end to the beginning and appending elements to a new list. Second, compare each element of the original array with the corresponding element in the reversed array. If all elements match, the array is a palindrome; otherwise, it is not. The time complexity is O(n) because we traverse the array twice—once to reverse it and once to compare—but these are linear operations. The space complexity is O(n) due to the additional array used for the reversed copy. This connects to L1 by building on the key subroutine of reversing the array. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like empty arrays or single-element arrays, which should return true as they trivially read the same forward and backward. Another mistake is comparing arrays incorrectly by using reference equality instead of element-wise comparison. To avoid this, ensure you iterate through both arrays and check each pair. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def is_palindrome(arr): reversed_arr = [] for i in range(len(arr)-1, -1, -1): reversed_arr.append(arr[i]) for i in range(len(arr)): if arr[i] != reversed_arr[i]: return False return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input array is [1, 2, 3, 2, 1]. Step 2: Create reversed array: iterate from index 4 to 0, appending elements: reversed_arr = [1, 2, 3, 2, 1]. Step 3: Compare original [1, 2, 3, 2, 1] with reversed [1, 2, 3, 2, 1]: index 0: 1 == 1, index 1: 2 == 2, index 2: 3 == 3, index 3: 2 == 2, index 4: 1 == 1. All match. Step 4: Return true.

Step 1: Input array is [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. Step 2: Create reversed array: iterate from index 4 to 0, appending elements: reversed_arr = [5, 4, 3, 2, 1]. Step 3: Compare original [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] with reversed [5, 4, 3, 2, 1]: index 0: 1 != 5. Step 4: Return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Reverse an Array
ladders.desc: Create a reversed copy of an array by iterating from the end to the beginning.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the reverse and compare approach, focusing on generating a reversed version of an input array. The intuition is that to check if an array is a palindrome, we need its mirror image for comparison, and reversing is the process of creating that mirror image by reading the array backwards. The algorithm involves initializing an empty list for the reversed array, then iterating through the original array from the last index to the first, appending each element to the new list. This ensures the order is inverted. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the array once, and space complexity is O(n) for the new array. This feeds into L0 by providing the reversed array needed for the comparison step. Common mistakes include incorrect iteration bounds, such as starting from len(arr) instead of len(arr)-1, or not handling empty arrays properly. To avoid, use a loop that goes from len(arr)-1 down to 0 inclusive. Real code pattern: def reverse_array(arr): reversed_arr = [] for i in range(len(arr)-1, -1, -1): reversed_arr.append(arr[i]) return reversed_arr.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input array is [1, 2, 3]. Step 2: Initialize reversed_arr = []. Step 3: Iterate i from 2 to 0: i=2, append arr[2]=3, reversed_arr=[3]; i=1, append arr[1]=2, reversed_arr=[3,2]; i=0, append arr[0]=1, reversed_arr=[3,2,1]. Step 4: Return [3, 2, 1].

Step 1: Input array is [a, b, c, d]. Step 2: Initialize reversed_arr = []. Step 3: Iterate i from 3 to 0: i=3, append arr[3]=d, reversed_arr=[d]; i=2, append arr[2]=c, reversed_arr=[d,c]; i=1, append arr[1]=b, reversed_arr=[d,c,b]; i=0, append arr[0]=a, reversed_arr=[d,c,b,a]. Step 4: Return [d, c, b, a].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compare Two Arrays Element-by-Element
ladders.desc: Check if two arrays are identical by comparing each corresponding element.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves verifying that two arrays are exactly the same by iterating through their indices and comparing elements at each position. The intuition is that for an array to be a palindrome, its reversed version must match the original element-for-element, so this comparison is essential to confirm equality. The algorithm starts by checking if the arrays have the same length; if not, they cannot be identical. Then, it loops through each index from 0 to len(arr)-1, comparing arr1[i] with arr2[i]. If any pair differs, the arrays are not identical; if all match, they are. Time complexity is O(n) for the loop, and space complexity is O(1) as no extra data structures are used. This connects to L1 by using the reversed array from that level as one input for comparison. Common mistakes include not checking array lengths first, which can lead to index errors or incorrect results if arrays differ in size. Also, using == for arrays might compare references rather than content in some languages, so element-wise comparison is safer. Real code pattern: def compare_arrays(arr1, arr2): if len(arr1) != len(arr2): return False for i in range(len(arr1)): if arr1[i] != arr2[i]: return False return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: arr1 length=3, arr2 length=3, so proceed. Step 2: Iterate i=0: arr1[0]=1, arr2[0]=1, match. i=1: arr1[1]=2, arr2[1]=2, match. i=2: arr1[2]=3, arr2[2]=3, match. Step 3: All match, return true.
Step 1: arr1 length=2, arr2 length=2, so proceed. Step 2: Iterate i=0: arr1[0]=a, arr2[0]=a, match. i=1: arr1[1]=b, arr2[1]=c, do not match. Step 3: Return false.

testCases.input: arr1=[a, b, c], arr2=[a, b, d]

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Through an Array Backwards
ladders.desc: Traverse an array from the last element to the first using a loop.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the technique of iterating through an array in reverse order, which is crucial for creating a reversed copy. The intuition is that to reverse an array, we need to access elements starting from the end, and this iteration pattern allows us to do so systematically. The algorithm uses a loop that starts at the last index (len(arr)-1) and decrements down to 0, accessing arr[i] at each step. This is a fundamental skill in array manipulation. Time complexity is O(n) for the traversal, and space complexity is O(1) if just iterating without storing. This connects to L2 by providing the backward iteration needed for reversing in the comparison context. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors, such as starting at len(arr) which is out of bounds, or stopping at 1 instead of 0. To avoid, ensure the loop condition is correct: for i in range(len(arr)-1, -1, -1). Real code pattern: def iterate_backwards(arr): for i in range(len(arr)-1, -1, -1): # process arr[i].

examples.trace: Step 1: Array length=3. Step 2: Loop i from 2 to 0: i=2, access arr[2]=3; i=1, access arr[1]=2; i=0, access arr[0]=1. Step 3: Output order: 3, 2, 1.

examples.output: Elements accessed: d, c, b, a
examples.trace: Step 1: Array length=4. Step 2: Loop i from 3 to 0: i=3, access arr[3]=d; i=2, access arr[2]=c; i=1, access arr[1]=b; i=0, access arr[0]=a. Step 3: Output order: d, c, b, a.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve the value at a specific position in an array using its index.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing an element in an array given its index, which is fundamental to all array manipulations. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and each element can be directly accessed via its position (index), starting from 0 for the first element. The algorithm is simple: use the syntax arr[index] to get the value. This is a constant-time operation, O(1), as it involves a direct memory lookup. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 by enabling the retrieval of elements during backward iteration. Common mistakes include using an index out of bounds (e.g., index >= len(arr) or index < 0), which can cause errors. To avoid, always ensure the index is within 0 and len(arr)-1. Real code pattern: element = arr[i] where i is a valid index.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array is [10, 20, 30]. Step 2: Index is 1. Step 3: Access arr[1] which is 20. Step 4: Output 20.
Step 1: Array is [a, b, c, d]. Step 2: Index is 3. Step 3: Access arr[3] which is d. Step 4: Output d.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is an Array?
ladders.desc: Understand that an array is an ordered collection of elements accessible by index.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces arrays as a basic data structure, essential for the palindrome problem. An array is a contiguous block of memory that stores multiple elements of the same type in a specific order. The intuition is that it allows efficient access to any element via its position (index), starting from 0. This ordered nature is key to checking palindromes, as we need to compare elements in sequence. Arrays have a fixed size in some languages or dynamic in others, but here we assume a list-like structure. Time and space are not analyzed at this level as it's conceptual. This connects to L4 by providing the context for element access. Common mistakes include confusing arrays with other data structures like sets or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. To avoid, practice creating and accessing simple arrays. Real code pattern: arr = [1, 2, 3] defines an array with elements 1, 2, 3.

examples.trace: Step 1: Define an array as a list of values. Step 2: Write arr = [5, 10, 15]. Step 3: This array has indices: 0->5, 1->10, 2->15.

examples.input: Access the second element of array [a, b, c]
examples.trace: Step 1: Array is [a, b, c]. Step 2: Indices: 0->a, 1->b, 2->c. Step 3: Second element corresponds to index 1. Step 4: Access arr[1] which is b.
